title: Harnessing Geospatial Technology for Early Childhood Development and Education in Zambia

indicators_measured.name: Participation rate in organized learning (one year before official primary entry age)
Proportion of children moderately or severely stunted
Proportion of population using safely managed drinking water services
Proportion of population using safely managed sanitation services

indicators_targeted.name: Pre-primary organized learning participation
indicators_targeted.rationale: Expanding equitable access to early childhood development and education (ECDE) infrastructure

indicators_targeted.name: Child stunting
indicators_targeted.rationale: ECDE sites integrate health, nutrition, WASH, and child protection services

indicators_targeted.name: Safely managed drinking water access
indicators_targeted.rationale: Site selection weights proximity to water and sanitation infrastructure

summary: UNICEF Zambia, with the Frontier Data Network and government partners (ZAMSTATS, GRID3), used geospatial analytics to identify optimal locations for new early childhood development and education (ECDE) centers funded by the EU Human Development Programme. The scoring model ranks potential sites by population density of target-age children, proximity to schools, health facilities, and road networks across 10 districts in 2 provinces, identifying 6,994 potential sites (~3,000 meeting criteria of >100 children near essential services). Data came from publicly available geospatial sources; automated backend ranking replaced labor-intensive manual map review. Community consultations, focus groups, and field visits validated sites and incorporated local knowledge (e.g., seasonal migration). Methods include GIS proximity analysis, weighted multi-criteria site scoring, stakeholder engagement, and iterative model refinement combining quantitative analytics with qualitative ground-truthing.
